title: Quikscript rune file
description: One rune per file: ductus (the closed enumeration of ways), stances with complete join surfaces, and policy riders. Stance identity is the structured tuple; generated display names never appear in authored data.

rune.description: The PostScript family name for this letter: `qsX` (e.g. `qsMay`), or an underscore-joined ligature like `qsTea_qsOy`. You author only this and the stances; the build derives every glyph's display name from them, so you never write one. To read a display name back: the rune, then the non-default stance, then `en-y<N>`/`ex-y<N>` for each live anchor (N is the height's y — baseline 0, x-height 5, y6 6, top 8), then `ex-wd` if the exit withdrew from the base bitmap, then any adjustment tokens (`en-ext-1`, `locked`). Capped at 63 bytes; an overflow hashes the tail. Example: `qsMay.grounded-loop.en-y5.ex-y0`.

codepoint.description: The Unicode code point for this rune's raw glyph. One per rune, mutually exclusive with `sequence` (ligatures use that instead). Example: ·Pea is `codepoint: 0xE650`.

sequence.description: Ligature runes only (instead of `codepoint`): the component families this single glyph stands in for, in order. Example: `sequence: [qsTea, qsOy]` — the ·Tea·Oy ligature `qsTea_qsOy`.

ductus.description: How each way is drawn, in prose, keyed by the way's pen-motion id — documentation for readers, never read by the build for geometry. Example: ·Pea declares `full` and `half`.

notes.description: Free-form prose about this rune, for human readers — never read by the build. Unlike `ductus` (how the letter is drawn), `notes` records join constraints and design rationale.

mono.description: The monospace (fixed-width) reference `bitmap` of this letter, with an optional `y_offset`. The proportional, contextual shapes live in `stances`.

stances.description: This letter's proportional shapes, keyed by stance name. Each entry is one stance — a `bitmap` plus its join `surface` — the contextual variants the build picks between, as against the single fixed-width `mono`. At least one is required, and policy records refer back to a stance by this name. A rune with exactly one stance names that sole stance `hapax`, the reserved sentinel for one-stance runes, with the pen motion still recorded in `way`.

policy.description:
Rules that change how this letter joins depending on which letters sit next to it.

The `surface` block above gives the join geometry that holds no matter the neighbors; `policy` is where the adjustments for specific neighbors go.

The riders: `order` (blunt default ranking), `refuse` (veto a join), `prefer` (break a tie), `extend`/`contract` (lengthen/shorten the connector), `resolve` (a hand-arbitrated conflict), `groups` (reusable named letter sets). Most carry a `when:` saying which neighbors they fire for.

familyOrList.description: A single family (`qsMay`) or a list of families (`[qsMay, qsUtter]`). Matches if any family matches.

strokeOrientation.description: A stroke orientation: `horizontal`, `vertical`, or `diagonal`. Matches when the neighbor presents this stroke at the seam.

boundaryValue.description: Matches a boundary rather than a letter. `namer-dot` matches just the namer dot; `boundary` matches the namer dot, space, ZWNJ, and the start or end of the text (where there's no glyph at all). Space and ZWNJ are deliberately not addressable on their own: they split runs, so what follows one is definitionally word-initial and what precedes one definitionally word-final (design section 3.4), and no record may render them differently from the same letters at a text edge. The namer dot stays addressable because it does not split runs.

bitmap.description: The letter's pixel grid: row strings top to bottom, `#` for ink and space for blank.

y_offset.description: Shifts this bitmap down by N pixels so a Deep letter reaches below the baseline. Deep letters use `-3`; everything else omits it (defaults to 0). Example: qsMay (a Deep letter) sets `y_offset: -3`.

way.description: The pen-motion this stance is drawn with — must be one of the rune's `ductus` keys. Stances can share a way.

traits.description: The stance's design flags — a closed set: `half` (shortened to the x-height) and `alt` (an alternate form). Omit for neither. Anything else that distinguishes a stance lives in named bitmaps or policy groups, not here. Example: qsPea's half stance — `traits: [half]`.

bitmap.description: The letter's pixel grid: row strings top to bottom, `#` for ink and space for blank.

y_offset.description: Shifts this stance's bitmap down by N pixels so a Deep letter reaches below the baseline. Deep letters use `-3`; everything else omits it (defaults to 0) — the same as `mono`'s `y_offset`.

bitmaps.description: Named alternate bitmaps this stance can swap to — each a full pixel redraw, as opposed to the default `bitmap`. Author one when a join needs a different shape that stub or extension arithmetic can't produce. The names are wired up elsewhere: a row's `joined`/`withdrawal` binding, a `cells.bitmap`, or a policy record's `bind` all refer to a bitmap by name to pull it in. Example: qsMay's loop stance carries a `pulled-back-stubless` bitmap (the entry stub tucked in), bound by its x-height entry when ·May lands joined there.

surface.description: This stance's join surfaces — how it connects to neighbors. `entries` are the left side: where a predecessor may join in, by height. `exits` are the right side: how this stance reaches toward the successor, by height. The rest constrain those: `pairings`, `cells`, `unlocks`, `require`.

entries.description: The left side: each height (baseline, x-height, y6, top) where a predecessor may join into this stance, mapped to an entry row.

exits.description: The right side: each height where this stance reaches toward the successor, mapped to an exit row.

pairings.description: Which seam height-combinations this letter will and won't make. `never` lists the pairs you forbid; `only` is the complete whitelist of the ones you allow — anything not listed is then off the table. Each side is a height (`baseline`, `x-height`, `y6`, `top`) or `none` when that side isn't joined. It only constrains a stance joined on both sides at once; one-sided and isolated forms always exist. An `unlocks` row can re-admit a pair this forbids. Example: ·May's loop sets `never: [{entry: baseline, exit: baseline}, {entry: x-height, exit: x-height}]` — it won't pass a stroke straight through at the same height.

cells.description:
Names the exact bitmap for a specific join state this stance settles into. A "cell" is one concrete entry-state-plus-exit-state combination. The build normally picks each cell's bitmap on its own — from an entry's `joined` binding, a declined exit's `withdrawal`, or the base bitmap; list a cell here only to override that, giving the state its own named `bitmap`. Each row is `{entry, exit, bitmap}`, where `entry`/`exit` are a height, `none`, or a height plus `-withdrawn` for an exit declined mid-word that pulled its ink back. Example: ·Pea's half, joined at the x-height on both sides, swaps in its both-ends dip — `{entry: x-height, exit: x-height, bitmap: half-dips-both-sides}`.

·Utter's `alternate`, entered at the x-height with its baseline exit withdrawn, swaps the reaching tail away — `{entry: x-height, exit: baseline-withdrawn, bitmap: reaches-way-back-withdrawn}`.

unlocks.description: Extra joins this stance gets only when a stylistic set is turned on. Each record names one feature (`ssNN`) and grants exactly one capability: an extra `entry` height, an extra `exit` height, or an extra `pairing` (entry+exit combo) — beyond what `entries`/`exits`/`pairings` allow by default. Add a `when:` to grant it only in context (same left-settled / right-raw rules as `from`). The new anchor's x comes from the base bitmap by convention, so the row must have ink there. (What each set is for is listed in the README's Stylistic sets section.) Example: ·It's baseline-to-baseline pairing opens whenever `ss04` is on — `{pairing: {entry: baseline, exit: baseline}, feature: ss04}`; a `when:` on that record would narrow the grant to specific neighbors instead.

require.description: Which sides must actually be joined for this stance to apply — `entry`, `exit`, or both. A required side that ends up unjoined drops the stance from the running, so it can only appear mid-join, never as an isolated or word-boundary form. Leave it `[]` for an ordinary stance that's free to stand alone. Example: ·Utter's `alternate` stance is a join-born connecting shape — `require: [exit]` keeps it from ever surfacing without a successor to reach toward.

x.description: The entry anchor's column at this height's row — conventionally the leftmost-ink column in that row. The build warns if it sits elsewhere; set `x_off_convention` to say it's deliberate.

stroke.description: The stroke orientation where a predecessor lands at this entry: `horizontal`, `vertical`, or `diagonal`. Declarative — it doesn't gate joins by itself; it's the value a `stroke:` condition can filter on.

from.description: Which left neighbors may join at this entry — a list of conditions; absent means anyone. The left neighbor is already settled here, so these can inspect its chosen `stance` and the seam height it landed at (`joined_at`) — axes the right-side `toward` can't use. Example: ·Pea's x-height entry takes ·May, ·Utter, and the ·Day·Utter ligature only when they land at the x-height.

joined.description:
The bitmap to use when a predecessor actually joins at this entry height — names one of this stance's own `bitmaps` (its named alternates) to swap in for the base bitmap. Only those names resolve: not the base `bitmap`, and not the rune's `mono`.

The entry-side mirror of an exit's `withdrawal`: `withdrawal` redraws when nothing joins, `joined` redraws when something does. Omit it to keep the base bitmap. Example: ·May's loop sets `joined: pulled-back-stubless` at the x-height.

joined_x.description: The entry anchor's column for the bitmap specified by `joined`. The row's `x` marks the leftmost ink of the stance's default bitmap; when the joined bitmap's leftmost ink lands in a different column, `joined_x` is that column instead. Omit when it doesn't change. Example: ·May's grounded loop drops its x-height entry from `x: 3` to `joined_x: 2` under `joined: pulled-back-grounded`.

selectable.description: Default `true`. Set `false` to emit an anchor for GPOS cursive-positioning parity while keeping the join engine from ever joining there — it changes no shaping, only the anchor table. Removing it leaves every settlement identical and just drops the parity anchors. Carried over from the old font's `entry_curs_only`; rarely needed on new work. Example: qsTea's `top: {x: 0, selectable: false}`.

x_off_convention.description: The build warns if an anchor isn't sitting in its usual spot. `x_off_convention: true` just means 'I put it there on purpose — skip the warning.' Nothing in the font uses it yet.

x.description: The exit anchor's column at this height's row — conventionally one column to the right of the rightmost ink in that row. The build warns if it lands elsewhere; set `x_off_convention` to say it's deliberate.

stroke.description: The stroke orientation this stance's exit presents at this height: `horizontal`, `vertical`, or `diagonal`. Declarative — it doesn't gate joins by itself; it's the value the following letter's entry `from` `stroke:` condition reads.

toward.description:
Which letters this exit may reach toward — a list of conditions; leave it off to allow anyone.

The right-side mirror of an entry's `from`, with one catch: the next letter isn't settled yet, so a condition here can only match who it is — `family`, `class`, `stroke`, or a boundary. It can't name the stance that letter will pick or the height it will join at; those are left-side-only, and the build errors if a `toward` uses them.

Example: ·Pea's half reaches its y6 exit only toward another ·Pea — `toward: [family: qsPea]`.

Its x-height exit shows the class form: any letter that can enter at the x-height, except ·Tea, ·Day, ·Fee, ·Ye, and ·Owe — `toward: [class: can-enter-at-x-height, except: [family: qsTea, …]]`.

withdrawal.description: What this exit looks like when it isn't joined — either the next letter doesn't join back, or this letter ends the word. `safe` claims the base bitmap already leaves no connector ink reaching at this row when nothing follows, and the build errors if it does. Otherwise, name one of this stance's own `bitmaps` to use instead — a version that doesn't leave that reaching ink, where the base bitmap does. As with `joined`, only those names resolve: not the base `bitmap`, and not the rune's `mono`. Example: ·Pea's baseline exit is `withdrawal: safe`. No rune names a bitmap here today — every declared withdrawal is `safe`; the one declined-exit redraw in the runes (·Utter's `reaches-way-back-withdrawn`) rides a `-withdrawn` cell instead.

ink_y.description: The row to scan for ink when the exit anchor's own row is blank, so the build still measures where the stroke really exits. Example: ·Pea's half form sets `ink_y: 6`.

x_off_convention.description: The build warns if an anchor isn't sitting in its usual spot. `x_off_convention: true` just means 'I put it there on purpose — skip the warning.' Nothing in the font uses it yet.

stub.description: A connector nub at this anchor row: bitmap column(s) that appear or vanish depending on whether the side joins. `cols` names the columns; `inks_when` says which state inks them.

cols.description: The bitmap column indices the nub occupies.

inks_when.description: The join state in which these stub columns are inked — a connector nub that blinks on with the seam. `joined` = blank in the base bitmap, inks when a neighbor joins this side; `withdrawn` = the reverse, ink that retracts on joining (no rune uses it). Example: qsPea's full x-height entry, `stub: {cols: [0], inks_when: joined}` — column 0 is blank until ·May or ·Utter joins, then a nub sprouts to catch the incoming stroke.

entry.description: The pair's left side: the height a predecessor joins in at (`baseline`, `x-height`, `y6`, `top`), or `none` for no join on that side.

exit.description: The pair's right side: the height this letter reaches its successor at, or `none` for no join on that side.

entry.description: The entry-side state this cell is for: the height a predecessor joined at, or `none` when nothing joined there.

exit.description: The exit-side state this cell is for: the height this letter joins onward at, `none` for no exit, or a height plus `-withdrawn` for an exit that was declined mid-word and pulled its ink back.

bitmap.description:
The bitmap this cell renders — one of this stance's own `bitmaps`.

As with `joined` and `withdrawal`, only those names resolve: not the base `bitmap`, and not the rune's `mono`. An explicit row here also settles the tie when the entry's `joined` and an exit's `withdrawal` name different bitmaps — the build stops and asks you to pick by writing this row.

entry_x.description:
Puts this cell's entry anchor in a different column — set it when the cell's bitmap has its entry-side ink somewhere other than where the row's `x` (or `joined_x`) says.

No rune uses it yet.

exit_x.description:
Puts this cell's exit anchor in a different column — set it when the cell's bitmap has its exit-side ink somewhere other than where the row's `x` says.

Example: ·Utter's `reaches-way-back` cell reaches further right, so its exit anchor moves out to `exit_x: 6`.

feature.description: The stylistic set (`ssNN`) that switches this unlock on — the grant applies only while a user has that set enabled.

entry.description: Grants an extra entry height while the feature is on. Exactly one of `entry`, `exit`, or `pairing` per unlock.

exit.description: Grants an extra exit height while the feature is on. Exactly one of `entry`, `exit`, or `pairing` per unlock.

pairing.description: Grants an extra entry+exit combination while the feature is on — a pair `pairings` would otherwise forbid. Exactly one of `entry`, `exit`, or `pairing` per unlock.

when.description: Narrows the grant to a context — the usual `when` rules apply (settled left, raw right). Without it, the unlock holds wherever the feature is on.

order.description: This rune's stances ranked by precedence, default first. The first stance is the one this letter wears in isolation and the base of its name; list every stance so the order is explicit. When two stances survive all the join reasoning and prefers in a tie, the earlier-listed one wins — this is a late, blunt tiebreaker, not a join condition, so reach for `prefer` or `refuse` when the choice actually depends on the neighbors. Any stance you omit is appended in declaration order.

groups.description: Named sets of letters this rune's own conditions can call up by name. Define one under a name, then reference it as `class: that-name` anywhere a `when:` takes families. It's for a trigger list this rune reuses across records — name it once instead of repeating the families. Shared, cross-rune sets belong in the script-wide predicate-class registry instead; these `groups` are private to this rune. Each group is a `union`/`minus` of families (see `groupDefinition`), and a `trait` or `stance` qualifier on a member is widened away to its whole family. Most runes need none and leave this `{}`. Example: a rune that vetoes the same predecessors across several records can name them once — `baseline-vetoes: {union: [family: qsZoo, family: qsShe]}` — and reuse the set as `except: [class: baseline-vetoes]`.

refuseRecord.description:
Refuses a join: when its `when` matches, the join this record names is off the table, and the build joins the letters some other way or not at all.

Narrow the target with `stance` and/or an `entry`/`exit` height to refuse only that one join — that stance, joining at that height. Name none of them and every join is refused: the letter sits unjoined whenever the `when` matches.

The `when` can read the settled left neighbor but only the raw right one.

Example: ·It refuses its x-height exit into the full ·Day's entry — `{stance: hapax, exit: x-height, when: {right: {family: qsDay}}, why: …}` — so ·It·Day joins low instead of through the forbidden same-height pass-through.

stance.description: Limits the refusal to this stance.

entry.description: Limits the refusal to joins that enter at this height.

exit.description: Limits the refusal to joins that exit at this height.

why.description: An optional note saying why this join is forbidden. It shows up in the build's elimination diagnostics but changes no shaping.

preferRecord.description: Tie-breaker records. When more than one stance survives a seam, a `prefer` record speaks for one so it wins. Each names what it favors — a whole `stance`, or a `cell` exit/entry pattern (with `over:` naming the loser) — under a `when` condition. `mode` decides how hard it pushes: `yields-to-joins` (the default) stands down whenever some other choice buys a better join with the neighbor, so it only settles genuine ties; `absolute` wins regardless and so demands a `why`. A record may live on this rune (favoring its own candidate) or on the follower (voting for the seam its own continuation joins best under). Example: ·No prefers its `flipped` stance after ·It (`stance: flipped`, `when: {left: {family: qsIt}}`) — both seam heights tie on join count in isolation, so the tie goes to the baseline join; because it yields to joins, ·It·No·Owe still takes the x-height path when that scores a second join.

extendRecord.description: Lengthen one side of this stance's seam stroke when the join matches — the inverse of `contract`. Name the side and height with `entry` (left) or `exit` (right) plus a height, then `by: N` to add N pixels: the stroke widens at that row and the neighbor slides over, so the connecting stroke gains N pixels of ink. `when:` gates which joins get it; left-side records can read the settled neighbor's `stance` and `joined_at`, right-side ones can't. Most-specific record wins, and extensions never sum on one side. `ok: [lo, hi]` is the band of total extension the build's seam check will accept (defaults to `[by, by]`); widen it when both sides of a seam can legitimately add up past `by`. `split` is reserved and unused. Example: ·May's loop adds a pixel of x-height exit toward ·Day/·Fee/·Jay and a few others — `{stance: loop, exit: x-height, by: 1, ok: [1, 1], when: {right: {family: [qsDay, qsDay_qsUtter, qsFee, qsJai, qsJay, qsRoe, qsIt]}}}`.

ok.description: The band of total extension the build's seam check will accept — `[lo, hi]`, defaulting to `[by, by]`. Widen it when extension from both sides of a seam can legitimately push the total past `by`.

split.description: Reserved and unused — parsed and modeled, but no rune sets it and nothing in the build consumes it.

contractRecord.description:
Shorten one side of a join for specific neighbors — the opposite of `extend`.

Aim it with `stance` plus `entry` or `exit` at a height, gated by `when:`. Then pick exactly one way to pull back: `by: N` shortens the connecting stroke by N pixels and moves the anchor in with it; `trim: N` blanks N pixels of ink but leaves the anchor where it was; `bind` swaps in one of this stance's own `bitmaps`, for a pull-back that spans rows and isn't simple arithmetic.

The most specific record wins per side, and a side's adjustment never sums.

Example: ·No's loop exit shortens by a pixel before ·J'ai — `{stance: loop, exit: x-height, by: 1, when: {right: {family: qsJai}}}` (·Utter's does the same by two). Only `by` is in use today; no rune has a `trim` or `bind` yet.

resolveRecord.description: A recorded tie-break for a conflict the engine refuses to guess on: when two policy records collide on the same seam and neither is more specific, the build stops with an error and you settle it here. Two shapes. `against` points at the specific competing record on another rune — `{rune: qsGay, id: plant-word-initial}` — and `pick` names the winner. `at` instead overrides the engine's default for a named window class — `{at: {right: {family: qsTea}}}`. Either way `why:` is required, since a resolve is a judgment call. `migrated:` tags one carried over wholesale from the old font, keeping that import debt visible. Every rune carries `resolve: []` today — you only add one when the build hands you an E-INCOMPARABLE or E-AMBIGUOUS conflict to break.

class.description: A named set of letters — either a group from this rune's `policy.groups`, or a script-wide predicate class. Matches if the letter is a member.

when.description:
The surroundings a record needs before it fires. List any of `left`, `right`, `self`, `word`, `feature`. The record fires only if all of them match.

`left` reads the already-settled left neighbor, so it can name the `stance` it chose and the height it `joined_at`. `right` reads the raw, not-yet-settled right neighbor, so it matches only static identity (`family`, `class`, `stroke`, boundary) — never a stance or a seam height.

`self` is this letter's own entry/exit state. `word` is its position in the run (initial, medial, final, isolated). `feature` is the active stylistic set (`ssNN`).

Example: ·Pea's full-way refuse uses `{left: {joined_at: y6}, right: {class: can-enter-at-x-height, except: […]}}`: a settled-left height beside a raw-right class.

word.description:
Where this letter sits in its word: `initial`, `medial`, `final`, or `isolated`.

A word breaks on a `space`, a `zwnj`, or the start or end of the text. A break on the left makes the letter `initial`, one on the right `final`, both `isolated`, neither `medial`.

The namer dot does not break a word — a letter beside one stays `medial` on that side, even though `is: namer-dot` still matches it as a neighbor.

whenWindowDecidable.description:
The surroundings this record needs before it fires.

List any of `left`, `right`, `self`, `word`, `feature`; the record fires only if all of them match.

right.description:
What the letter to the right must be for this record to fire.

List any of `family`, `class`, `stroke`, `is`, `except`.

`then` cannot be used here because it looks one letter beyond its right neighbor.

leftCondition.description:
What the left neighbor must be for this to match. Pin it down by `family`, by `class` (a named group), or by the exact `stance` it settled into; by the height it `joined_at`; by its exit `stroke`; or by `is` for a boundary like a space. `except` lists cases that make it fail instead.

Only the left side can name `stance` and `joined_at`, because the left neighbor is already settled by the time this letter is placed (see `when`).

class.description: A named set of letters — either a group from this rune's `policy.groups`, or a script-wide predicate class. Matches if the letter is a member.

stance.description: The variant the left neighbor settled into. A left-only axis (see `when`).

joined_at.description: The seam height the left neighbor settled at (`baseline`, `x-height`, `y6`, `top`, or `none`). Usable only on the left — the right neighbor isn't settled yet, so it has no seam height to name.

except.description: Drops neighbors from the match. A neighbor listed here is skipped even when the condition's other keys fit. On its own it's a plain negation — `left: {except: [family: qsIt]}` matches any left neighbor but ·It.

class.description: A named set of letters — either a group from this rune's `policy.groups`, or a script-wide predicate class. Matches if the letter is a member.

except.description: Drops neighbors from the match. A neighbor listed here is skipped even when the condition's other keys fit. On its own it's a plain negation — `left: {except: [family: qsIt]}` matches any left neighbor but ·It.

then.description:
Lets one join's decision account for the join just beyond it: it looks past the immediate right neighbor to the letter after that.

Only `prefer` may use it. `prefer` just breaks ties between surviving stances, so a wider look is harmless; `refuse` and `require` judge only the join in front of them, never the letter beyond.

Inside `then`, only the raw right axes apply (`family`, `class`, `stroke`, `is`, `except`), and it can't nest — the peek stops at that second letter.

Example: ·Utter's `prefer` picks `{exit: none}` over `{exit: x-height}` under `{right: {family: qsTea, then: {family: [qsDay, qsMay, qsIt, qsUtter]}}}` — a ·Tea joined at the x-height can't join onward, so when one of those four follows, ·Utter stands down and leaves ·Tea free to join them instead (·Utter·Tea·Day).

class.description: A named set of letters — either a group from this rune's `policy.groups`, or a script-wide predicate class. Matches if the letter is a member.

except.description: Drops neighbors from the match. A neighbor listed here is skipped even when the condition's other keys fit. On its own it's a plain negation — `left: {except: [family: qsIt]}` matches any left neighbor but ·It.
